new_findings.title: Commune of probable infection assignment protocol unspecified pre-Ñuble split
new_findings.evidence: cases reported before that date (n = 103) were originally registered under the SEREMI Biobío regional office and were assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
new_findings.mechanism: 103/136 (75.7%) cases rely on a reconciliation protocol relegated to Supplementary Methods without stating whether the protocol existed contemporaneously or was applied retrospectively. If retrospective, it introduces recall/records bias. If the Biobío SEREMI used residence (not infection site) pre-2018, systematic spatial misclassification would inflate/deflate commune-specific IRRs in the training period, biasing spatial random effects and FSI coefficients trained on those counts.
new_findings.fix_hint: Move reconciliation protocol to main text Methods (±100 words). State explicitly: (a) whether infection-site geolocation was collected prospectively by Biobío SEREMI 2002-2017 or assigned retrospectively; (b) what fraction of n=103 had field investigation reports vs residence-only records; (c) sensitivity analysis excluding cases with residence≠infection or limiting to post-2018 Ñuble-native registration.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Prior vault references case reconciliation and probable commune of infection protocol, but no Q1 citation provided, no numeric calculation shown, and no explicit integrated sentence in manuscript.

new_findings.title: FSI wildfire-veto threshold (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) lacks calibration evidence
new_findings.evidence: with active-burn pixels vetoed (ΔNBR2 < −0.15) to prevent wildfire-scar contamination (Ecke et al. 2019)
new_findings.mechanism: The ΔNBR2 = −0.15 threshold is cited to Ecke 2019 (a Scandinavian rodent study), but Ñuble's Mediterranean-climate wildfires have different burn severity distributions. If the threshold is too liberal, undetected fire scars remain in FSI, creating spurious negative correlations (burned forest → low FSI → high apparent risk). If too strict, it masks true C. quila post-fire regrowth signals. No sensitivity analysis across thresholds [−0.10, −0.20] is reported.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report fire-pixel prevalence in the Ñuble FSI series (% months affected). Perform sensitivity analysis varying ΔNBR2 ∈ {−0.10, −0.12, −0.15, −0.18, −0.20} and show IRR_R5 stability. If IRR sign flips, this undermines the ecological interpretation; if stable, cite Ñuble-specific justification or mark as exploratory calibration.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Ecke 2019 citation present, ΔNBR2 threshold −0.15 documented in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md and project memory S51, but no explicit calibration validation or integrated sentence in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Achibueno FSI calibration site selection bias (single-watershed pre-freeze)
new_findings.evidence: calibrated in Achibueno and frozen before migration to Ñuble (anti-circularity)
new_findings.mechanism: Achibueno is cited as the external calibration watershed, but no rationale for its selection is given. If Achibueno was chosen post-hoc because it had known hantavirus cases or visible C. quila signals, the 'anti-circularity' claim is undermined by ex post site selection. The FSI formula (NDMI+NDVI+NBR2 weights) derived from Achibueno may not generalize to Ñuble's coastal vs interior quiloide ecotones (Ward zones), introducing spatial transferability bias.
new_findings.fix_hint: State Achibueno selection criteria (geographic proximity? independent case data? literature precedent?) and show FSI correlation with known C. quila flowering events in that watershed. Perform split-sample validation: train FSI weights in Achibueno, test in a held-out Ñuble commune subset, report out-of-sample IRR_R5 to confirm transferability before full-panel application.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Q1 anti-circularity protocol documented in Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, Achibueno-freeze procedure explicitly described in GEE_FSI_Reconstruccion_S51.md, integrated in manuscript.

new_findings.title: CONAF seasonal patrol coverage (Oct-Apr) confounds operational risk window
new_findings.evidence: CONAF patrol coverage is seasonal (October–April), only partially overlapping the primary risk window
new_findings.mechanism: CONAF's seasonal patrol schedule (Oct–Apr) aligns with the HCPS seasonal peak (91.2% cases Oct–May). If CONAF access restrictions are only enforced during patrol months, observed case reductions in that window could reflect patrol-driven ascertainment bias (reduced access → fewer infected individuals → fewer diagnosed cases) rather than true ecological risk reduction. This confounds the FSI→risk pathway and inflates apparent model skill during validation folds that include Oct–Apr months.
new_findings.fix_hint: Test for differential FSI effect by patrol season: fit an interaction term FSI_R5 × I(month ∈ Oct–Apr). If IRR differs significantly, stratify skill metrics by patrol vs non-patrol months. Alternatively, limit operational claims to year-round municipal-SEREMI coordination and note CONAF seasonality as a coverage limitation, not a validation threat.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: CONAF Oct-Apr patrol coverage documented in Auditoria_Q1_S50_cierre.md, overlap with risk window explicitly noted in Paper_EID_Final.md, integrated in manuscript limitation section.

new_findings.title: 87% peridomestic transmission assumption (Riquelme 2015) unverified in Ñuble
new_findings.evidence: 87% peridomestic transmission per Riquelme 2015 supports spatial accuracy
new_findings.mechanism: Riquelme 2015 is cited to justify commune-level spatial accuracy under the infection-site assignment protocol, but the 87% figure likely derives from national-level or different-region data (not Ñuble-specific 2002–2024). Ñuble has distinct occupational patterns (forestry, agriculture) and settlement density; if the true peridomestic fraction is lower (e.g., 60–70% due to higher sylvatic/occupational exposure), commune-of-infection assignment loses precision, increasing non-differential misclassification and attenuating IRR_R5 toward the null.
new_findings.fix_hint: Report Ñuble-specific peridomestic fraction from field investigation records (if available) or sensitivity analysis: simulate misclassification under 60%, 75%, 87% peridomestic scenarios, re-estimate IRR_R5, and show CI overlap. If unavailable, downgrade the '87% supports accuracy' claim to 'national data suggest majority peridomestic; residual misclassification expected to bias toward null.'
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Riquelme 2015 citation present, 87% peridomestic transmission documented in multiple vault files, but no Ñuble-specific validation calculation or integrated caveat sentence in manuscript.

new_findings.title: Case reconciliation protocol in Supplementary Methods creates verifiability barrier
new_findings.evidence: following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods
new_findings.mechanism: The protocol governing 75.7% of cases (103/136 pre-Ñuble split) is not in the main text. Reviewers and readers cannot assess whether infection-site assignment was prospective (high validity) or retrospective chart review (subject to recall bias, missing geolocation data, residence-infection conflation). This is an information bias: the measurement process is described, but its quality is unverifiable without accessing supplementary materials that may not be peer-reviewed to the same standard.
new_findings.fix_hint: Promote the reconciliation protocol summary to main Methods (5.5.1), including: (a) data source (field reports vs medical records vs SEREMI administrative logs), (b) geolocation completeness (% with GPS coordinates vs residence proxy), (c) inter-rater reliability if retrospective assignment was performed. Reserve detailed flowcharts for Supplementary, but ensure core validity metrics are in main text.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Case reconciliation protocol referenced in vault Panel Oficial M1M2.md and Estado_Conocimiento_Serie_Ecologica_S50.md, but no Q1 citation for reconciliation method and no integrated phrase.

new_findings.title: Commune aggregation masks within-commune quiloide heterogeneity
new_findings.evidence: No inference is at the comuna–month panel level... Ecological fallacy (comuna-month aggregation carries inherent individual-level inference limits per Robinson 1950; Morgenstern 1995)
new_findings.mechanism: Ñuble communes vary 10–100× in area; within large communes (e.g., Coihueco 1,153 km²), C. quila patches are spatially heterogeneous. The FSI is spatially averaged across the entire commune, diluting focal quiloide signals. If a 10 km² high-risk quiloide patch exists within a 1,000 km² commune, the FSI signal is attenuated 100-fold, biasing IRR_R5 toward the null and underestimating true ecological associations.
new_findings.fix_hint: Perform sensitivity analysis at sub-commune resolution: (a) mask FSI to only forested pixels >800 m elevation (quiloide zone proxy); (b) compute 90th-percentile FSI within each commune instead of mean; (c) re-estimate IRR_R5 and compare CI width. If IRR strengthens materially (e.g., from 0.73 to 0.55), the averaging dilution is substantial and should be noted as a conservative bias.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Robinson 1950 and Morgenstern 1995 citations present, ecological fallacy explicitly discussed in GLMM NB2 Final.md and Blindajes G1-G6.md, integrated in manuscript limitations.

new_findings.title: Companion clinical study ethics approval overlap creates selective reporting risk
new_findings.evidence: both manuscripts were designed as complementary analyses under ICMJE criteria for acceptable secondary publication, with full disclosure to both journals... share no analytical methods, tables, figures, or computed metrics; only publicly available regional descriptors (total case count, case-fatality ratio, ethics approval) are common
new_findings.mechanism: The companion clinical study (Contreras et al., Rev Chil Infect) analyzes 34 HCPS patients 2010–2025, a subset of the 136 ecological cases. If negative ecological findings (e.g., non-significant lags, unexpected IRR signs) were selectively partitioned into the clinical paper to preserve the EWS narrative, this constitutes outcome reporting bias. The 'no shared metrics' claim prevents verification of whether all pre-specified ecological analyses appear in this manuscript or were moved elsewhere.
new_findings.fix_hint: In Supplementary Methods, list all pre-specified analyses from Zenodo v1.0/v1.1/v1.2 and cross-reference where each appears (EID ms vs RCI ms vs 'not pursued'). If any lag profiles, spatial analyses, or sensitivity tests were computed but not reported here, state the disposition (e.g., 'lag 0–4 and 6–10 reported in Supplementary Fig S2; clinical subgroup analyses in companion RCI manuscript').
new_findings.mcc_rationale: ICMJE secondary publication criteria documented in project_dual_manuscript_factory_S44.md, complementary analysis statement integrated in Paper_EID_Final.md and Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60.md.

new_findings.title: PAHO 2025 alert citation in Conclusions lacks methodological connection
new_findings.evidence: Against the regional backdrop of the PAHO December 2025 epidemiological alert documenting renewed hantavirus activity across the Americas (PAHO 2025), the work supports adoption...
new_findings.mechanism: The PAHO 2025 alert is invoked to frame urgency but appears only in the final sentence of Conclusions, without analysis of whether the 'renewed activity' is detectable in Ñuble's 2024 data or validation folds. If the alert reflects outbreaks in other countries (Argentina, Bolivia) with different hantavirus species (e.g., Laguna Negra) or ecology (Chaco vs Andean), citing it creates false relevance bias—implying the Ñuble EWS generalizes to PAHO-wide contexts without empirical support.
new_findings.fix_hint: Either (a) analyze whether Ñuble 2023–2024 case counts are elevated vs the Bortman threshold and explicitly link this to the PAHO alert, or (b) remove the PAHO citation from Conclusions and place it in Introduction/Discussion as context for regional surveillance gaps, without claiming the current model addresses PAHO-wide needs.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: PAHO 2025 citation present, multiple vault references to Manuscrito_v5_CONDENSED_S60.md, but no methodological connection calculation and no explicit sentence linking alert to study design.

new_findings.title: Zone factor dropped for 'redundancy' without testing zone×FSI interaction
new_findings.evidence: Zone factor was dropped for redundancy with the comuna random intercept (LRT p = 0.186; Hodges & Reich 2010)
new_findings.mechanism: The manuscript states zone (coastal vs interior quiloide binary) was dropped due to LRT p = 0.186, but does not test whether zone moderates the FSI effect (zone × FSI_R5 interaction). If coastal communes have different C. quila phenology (maritime influence) vs interior Andean foothills, the IRR_R5 could differ by zone. Dropping the main effect does not rule out effect modification, and the final model assumes FSI operates identically across both ecotones.
new_findings.fix_hint: Fit a sensitivity model including zone × FSI_R5 interaction and report the LRT p-value. If p > 0.10, the no-interaction assumption is supported. If p < 0.10, report stratum-specific IRRs (coastal vs interior) and discuss whether the single-IRR interpretation holds or requires geographic qualification.
new_findings.mcc_rationale: Hodges & Reich 2010 citation present, LRT p=0.186 documented in GLMM NB2 Final.md and Paper_EID_Final.md, zone-factor redundancy explicitly stated in manuscript model selection section.

new_objections.title: Retrospective case assignment to probable commune of infection violates temporal integrity
new_objections.critique: Pre-2018 cases (n=103, 76% of sample) were 'assigned to probable commune of infection' from BioBío records after administrative partition. This introduces post-hoc spatial re-allocation based on criteria not described, creating circular risk: if FSI informed assignment, the predictor is trained on its own output. The walk-forward protocol cannot protect against pre-fold contamination of historical labels.
new_objections.suggested_response: Perform sensitivity analysis restricting to post-2018 cases (n=33) where commune assignment is prospective. If model fails or CIs widen substantially, acknowledge geographic re-allocation as unmeasured confounding. Document assignment algorithm with blinding証明 or exclude pre-partition cases entirely.

new_objections.title: E-value 2.07 is weak and insufficient for causal inference from observational design
new_objections.critique: E-value=2.07 for FSI lag-5 (IRR=0.734) means an unmeasured confounder associated 2.07-fold with both FSI and HCPS could nullify the result. Given rodent abundance (the true mechanistic mediator) is unmeasured, this threshold is easily crossed. Claiming 'causal' lead time without rodent trapping data or instrumental-variable design is unjustified.
new_objections.suggested_response: Reframe as predictive association, not causal pathway. State explicitly: 'Without direct rodent abundance data, FSI→HCPS may be confounded by unmeasured ecological drivers. E-value 2.07 indicates moderate robustness but does not establish causality.' Replace 'enables preventive action' with 'generates risk signals for hypothesis-driven field validation.'

new_objections.title: Ranked probability score 'skill +1.3%' is null result misrepresented as directional evidence
new_objections.critique: RPS 0.038 vs null 0.038 with CI overlap is explicitly reported as 'not statistically distinguishable,' yet abstract includes RPS in the results summary without this caveat, and discussion claims 'triple scoring rule' validation. A null result on one of three metrics weakens—not strengthens—the evidentiary triangle. This is selective emphasis.
new_objections.suggested_response: Remove RPS from abstract. In discussion, state: 'RPS showed no detectable improvement over climatology (95% CI includes null), limiting support to the binary tier structure. Multi-tier ordinal skill requires larger N or external validation.' Reframe 'triple' as 'dual confirmatory (log, BSS) plus exploratory (RPS).'

new_objections.title: Decision curve analysis threshold range [0.05,0.20] lacks clinical/operational justification
new_objections.critique: The 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05,0.20]' is asserted without explanation. DCA requires threshold choice to reflect actual decision context: cost of false alarm vs missed case. No cost-effectiveness data, stakeholder survey, or budget constraint anchors this range. Arbitrary bounds allow cherry-picking the net-benefit peak.
new_objections.suggested_response: Either: (1) cite MINSAL or CONAF resource-allocation documents specifying acceptable false-positive rates, or (2) show DCA across full [0,0.5] range and mark [0.05,0.20] as post-hoc 'illustrative' only. State that threshold calibration requires prospective pilot deployment with observed intervention costs before operational use.

new_objections.title: Burn-in rationale cites time-series CV literature but model is spatial panel, not pure time series
new_objections.critique: Bergmeir 2018 and Cerqueira 2020 address univariate/multivariate time-series CV. Your model is a spatial panel GLMM with 21 clusters and within/between decomposition—different dependence structure. Citing time-seriesburn-in precedent for a mixed-model walk-forward is a category error. GLMMs can converge poorly with small N, but that's a maximum-likelihood issue, not time-series stationarity.
new_objections.suggested_response: Replace time-series citations with mixed-model references (e.g., Maas & Hox 2005 on minimum cluster counts). Justify burn-in via 'insufficient training events for stable θ estimation (n<50 per fold)' rather than non-stationarity. Show fold-wise θ and σ²_u trajectories in supplement to demonstrate convergence empirically.

new_candidates.title: Short-Term Management of Water-Damage Claim Risk Using Ensemble Precipitation Forecasts
new_candidates.abstract: Abstract. Insurers are increasingly challenged by weather-related claims arising from property damage, yet they lack adequate tools for near-term planning because traditional actuarial models do not incorporate real-time weather forecasts. This study demonstrates that incorporating ensemble precipitation forecasts improves short-range (1–4 days ahead) predictions of property insurance claim counts, thereby enabling proactive risk management. We present a forecasting framework for two Norwegian cities, Bergen and Oslo, using precipitation forecasts to predict days exceeding operationally significant thresholds. The models are evaluated by their forecast skill and reliability in predicting claim surges, as well as by their economic value in a cost-loss decision context, illustrating the pote
new_candidates.reason: Ensemble forecasts + proper scoring (cost-loss) + 1-4 day operational prediction – exactly mirrors our FSI->HCPS warning logic

new_candidates.title: ScoringBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Tabular Foundation Models with Proper Scoring Rules
new_candidates.abstract: Tabular foundation models such as TabPFN and TabICL already produce full predictive distributions yet prevailing regression benchmarks evaluate them almost exclusively via point estimate metrics RMSE R2 These aggregate measures often obscure model performance in the tails of the distribution a critical deficit for high stakes decision making in domains like finance and clinical research where asymmetric risk profiles are the norm We introduce ScoringBench an open benchmark that computes a comprehensive suite of proper scoring rules like CRPS CRLS Interval Score Energy Score weighted CRPS and Brier Score alongside standard point metrics providing a richer picture of probabilistic forecast quality We evaluate realTabPFNv2.5 fine tuned with different scoring rule objectives and TabICL relativ
new_candidates.reason: ScoringBench benchmark using CRPS/Brier/Interval Score for probabilistic model evaluation – supports our scoring rule choice

new_candidates.title: Improving Probabilistic Lightning Forecasts Through Ensemble Postprocessing with Mesoscale Information
new_candidates.abstract: Accurate short-term lightning forecasting requires reliable representations of both lightning occurrence and intensity, as well as the underlying convective processes. While ensemble prediction systems (EPSs) provide valuable probabilistic information, their ability to resolve mesoscale and convective-scale variability remains limited. In this study, we assess the added value of mesoscale information for probabilistic lightning forecasting over eastern China. A mesoscale ensemble is constructed from deterministic forecasts of the China Meteorological Administration (CMA) Mesoscale Model (MESO) using spatiotemporal neighborhood and time-lagged techniques and is combined with predictors from the CMA Regional Ensemble Prediction System (REPS). Lightning occurrence and counts are modeled withi
new_candidates.reason: Lightning EPS + mesoscale ensemble + probabilistic calibration – parallel early-warning system with lagged predictors

new_candidates.title: TimeSeek: Temporal Reliability of Agentic Forecasters
new_candidates.abstract: We introduce TimeSeek, a benchmark for studying how the reliability of agentic LLM forecasters changes over a prediction market's lifecycle. We evaluate 10 frontier models on 150 CFTC-regulated Kalshi binary markets at five temporal checkpoints, with and without web search, for 15,000 forecasts total. Models are most competitive early in a market's life and on high-uncertainty markets, but much less competitive near resolution and on strong-consensus markets. Web search improves pooled Brier Skill Score (BSS) for every model overall, yet hurts in 12% of model-checkpoint pairs, indicating that retrieval is helpful on average but not uniformly so. Simple two-model ensembles reduce error without surpassing the market overall. These descriptive results motivate time-aware evaluation and select
new_candidates.reason: TimeSeek: temporal reliability + Brier Skill Score + model performance at lifecycle checkpoints – validates BSS use

new_candidates.title: Geometric-Topological Quantum-AI for Continuous Wearable Biosensing, Physiological State Mapping, and Early Cardiometabolic Risk Prediction
new_candidates.abstract: Abstract: This study investigates whether a geometric-topological Quantum-AI framework can improve early cardiometabolic risk prediction from continuous wearable biosensing streams. The manuscript integrates wrist-derived heart rate, nocturnal heart rate variability, daily step count, sleep duration, sleep regularity, and oxygen saturation into a physiological state-space representation that is treated as a curved manifold rather than a flat feature table. Topological summaries are extracted to preserve recurrent and transitional structure in physiological trajectories, while a quantum-kernel classifier is used to encode nonlinear interactions between autonomic, metabolic, and behavioural signatures that are difficult to separate with conventional clinical scores. The study is written in t
new_candidates.reason: Wearable biosensing + state-space + early risk prediction + calibration – conceptual parallel for lagged physiological signals

new_candidates.title: Earth System Foundation Models for Predicting Extreme Climate Events Through Integrated Satellite, Oceanic, and Land-Surface Intelligence
new_candidates.abstract: Abstract: This study investigates the scientific and operational potential of Earth system foundation models for anticipating extreme climate events through the joint assimilation and representation of atmospheric, oceanic, and land-surface information. The manuscript develops a rigorous synthesis framework built on real observational and reanalysis data sources, including ERA5, SMAP, NOAA OISST v2.1, GPM IMERG, MODIS surface products, and the NOAA IBTrACS archive. Rather than treating weather, ocean, and land processes as isolated forecasting domains, the proposed framework formalizes extreme-event prediction as a multiscale sequence-learning problem in which precursor signals propagate across coupled geophysical subsystems. Particular attention is given to rapidly intensifying tropical c
new_candidates.reason: Earth system precursor signals + satellite indices + extreme events + multi-scale – reinforces FSI satellite approach

new_candidates.title: Satellite-Driven Machine Learning Approaches for Rainfall Forecasting in Support of Smart Irrigation: The Case of Ankara and Samsun
new_candidates.abstract: Accurate rainfall forecasting is essential for agriculture, water management, and disaster preparedness, and its importance is further amplified by increasing climate variability. Traditional approaches often fail to adequately capture the nonlinear and highly variable nature of rainfall, particularly at the local scale. This study investigates the effectiveness of machine learning techniques for daily rainfall prediction in Ankara and Samsun, Türkiye, to support smart irrigation systems. Rainfall time series derived from the PERSIANN satellite dataset (2020–2024) were enriched with lagged values, rolling statistics, dry-spell indices, and seasonal encodings. Multiple models, including Linear Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Regression (SVR), Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost, were c
new_candidates.reason: Satellite rainfall ML + lagged features + smart irrigation – similar lagged remote-sensing architecture for operational use

new_candidates.title: A machine-learning framework for virus early warning from weather and urban sustainability signals
new_candidates.abstract: Cities shape mosquito and arboviral risk through the way heat, water, vegetation, and drainage are managed. We present a fully reproducible early-warning pipeline that predicts trap&ndash;week West Nile virus (WNV) positivity one to two weeks ahead using programmatically accessible sources (Socrata WNV pools and Meteostat weather), and a graph-aware post-hoc smoothing that enforces spatial coherence of predicted risks. Because direct API access to the Chicago portal requires a Socrata App Token, all reported experiments use an offline, synthetic trap&ndash;week panel that mirrors a Chicago-like spatio-seasonal structure, while we ship turn-key code to re-run the identical analysis on the real data once a token is provided. Across forward-chaining evaluations, gradient boosting with
new_candidates.reason: WNV early warning w/ weather lag, API data, spatial smoothing, gradient boosting—direct parallel to our FSI-HCPS pipeline

new_candidates.title: Mathematical Analysis Methods for Quantitative Scenario Generation of Renewable Power Output: A Comprehensive Review
new_candidates.abstract: As the proportion of renewable power continues to increase, its inherent intermittency and volatility pose serious challenges to the security and stability of power systems. Scenario generation technology serves as a key tool supporting decision-making methods such as stochastic optimization and risk analysis. By generating representative power output scenarios, it can effectively characterize the uncertainty of renewable power output. This paper systematically reviews mainstream methods for the scenario generation of renewable power output, categorizing them into two major classes: sampling-based methods and model-based methods. Among them, sampling-based methods include Monte Carlo sampling, Latin hypercube sampling (LHS), Markov chains (MCs), and Copula functions. Model-based methods en
new_candidates.reason: Scenario generation for stochastic optimization under uncertainty—conceptual parallel for FSI lag uncertainty

new_candidates.title: Cost-Aware Network Traffic Anomaly Detection with Histogram-Based Gradient Boosting
new_candidates.abstract: Intrusion Detection Systems (IDSs) operate under asymmetric misclassification costs: false alarms (FP) consume analysts’ time and erode trust, whereas missed attacks (FN) carry business risks. This paper presents a complete pipeline for network anomaly detection on the CIC-IDS2017 dataset using Histogram-Based Gradient Boosting (HGB), with a particular focus on cost-aware threshold selection on a validation split for representative operating regimes wFP:wFN∈{1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, 1:10}—treated as scenario-based proxies for varying risk posture, attack severity, and analyst workload rather than as universally fixed costs—and on the role of isotonic calibration. The results indicate that (i) under 1:1, the cost-optimal operating point aligns with the F1/MCC optimum; (ii) for 1:k cost regi
new_candidates.reason: Cost-aware threshold selection and calibration with gradient boosting, relevant to operational decision tiers

new_candidates.title: Weighted Soft-Voting Ensembles for Liver Disease Prediction: A Large-Scale Comparative Study with Transparent Evaluation
new_candidates.abstract: Early detection of liver disease can significantly improve patient outcomes and reduce healthcare costs. This study presents a comparative evaluation of four traditional machine learning classifiers—Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines, Gaussian Naïve Bayes, and a Multi-Layer Perceptron—alongside an enhanced weighted soft-voting ensemble model. Using a large, publicly available clinical dataset (~30,000 records), a fully nested, leakage-free cross-validation framework is employed to ensure robust and reliable evaluation. The proposed ensemble assigns adaptive weights based on per-fold model performance and demonstrates superior discrimination and calibration compared to individual classifiers. The results highlight the contribution of transparent ensemble modeling in achieving accu
new_candidates.reason: Nested cross-validation + calibration for prediction; reinforces our leakage-free evaluation + probability calibration methods
